examples.title: Display Environment Variables
examples.description: Demonstrates various commands to display environment variables
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Display all environment variables
env

# Display specific variable
echo "Current USER: $USER"
echo "Current HOME: $HOME"
echo "Current PATH: $PATH"

# Using printenv
printenv PWD
printenv SHELL
examples.explanation: This script shows different ways to display environment variables using 'env', 'echo', and 'printenv' commands.

examples.title: Set and Export Variables
examples.description: Demonstrates setting shell variables vs environment variables
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Create shell variable (local only)
MY_VAR="local_only"

# Create and export environment variable
export ENV_VAR="environment_variable"

# Export existing variable
export ANOTHER_VAR="now_environment"

# Display using env command
env | grep -E "(MY_VAR|ENV_VAR|ANOTHER_VAR)"

# Demonstrate child process visibility
bash -c 'echo "MY_VAR in child: $MY_VAR"'
echo "ENV_VAR in child: $ENV_VAR"
examples.explanation: Shows that shell variables (MY_VAR) are not visible to child processes, while exported environment variables (ENV_VAR, ANOTHER_VAR) are inherited.

examples.title: PATH Modification Script
examples.description: Demonstrates modifying PATH variable to add custom directories
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Display current PATH
echo "Current PATH: $PATH"

# Add custom directory to PATH (temporary for this session)
export PATH=$PATH:/home/user/myprograms/bin

# Verify new PATH
echo "New PATH: $PATH"

# Check if command exists in new directory
if [ -d "/home/user/myprograms/bin" ]; then
 echo "Custom directory added to PATH"
fi

# To make permanent, add to ~/.bashrc:
# echo 'export PATH=$PATH:/home/user/myprograms/bin' >> ~/.bashrc
# source ~/.bashrc
examples.explanation: Demonstrates how to temporarily modify PATH and explains how to make changes permanent by adding to .bashrc.

examples.title: Check Command Location
examples.description: Uses environment variables to locate commands in PATH
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Function to find command location
find_command() {
 cmd=$1
 for dir in $(echo $PATH | tr ':' ' '); do
 if [ -x "$dir/$cmd" ]; then
 echo "Found: $dir/$cmd"
 return 0
 fi
 done
 echo "Command '$cmd' not found in PATH"
 return 1
}

# Find different commands
echo "Finding 'ls':"
find_command "ls"

echo "Finding 'gcc':"
find_command "gcc"

echo "Current shell: $SHELL"
echo "Current directory: $PWD"
examples.explanation: Demonstrates how PATH is used to locate executables and shows other important environment variables like SHELL and PWD.
